text: The surgeon identifies the intraperitoneal appendix with small bowel adhered to its lateral aspect. The base of the cecum is visible, and the surgeon carefully separates the small bowel from the appendix to elevate it.

The surgeon identifies the intraperitoneal appendix with small bowel adhered to it. The base of the cecum is visible, and the surgeon gently separates the small bowel from the lateral aspect of the appendix to elevate it.

The surgeon begins freeing the appendix by lifting it straight in the air to identify the base. The medial aspect of the appendiceal mesentery is visible, and the lateral aspect is being separated from the retroperitoneum, which has formed a phlegmon.

The surgeon frees the appendix by carefully lifting it from the retroperitoneum, which has formed a phlegmon. The medial aspect of the appendiceal mesentery is visible, along with the lateral aspect beneath the body of the appendix.

The surgeon carefully exposes the junction of the appendix to the cecum, ensuring clear visibility, especially due to potential secondary inflammation involving the cecum. The body of the appendix is then lifted out from the surrounding phlegmon.

The surgeon exposes the junction of the appendix to the cecum, ensuring clear visibility despite secondary inflammation. The body of the appendix is carefully extracted from the surrounding phlegmon tissue.

The surgeon continues to dissect the remaining appendiceal mesentery, revealing a possible iatrogenic perforation near the base of the appendix. A small portion of mesentery is still visible and needs to be removed.

The surgeon continues to dissect the remaining appendiceal mesentery. A small iatrogenic perforation is visible near the base of the appendix, with a small portion of mesentery still needing to be removed.

The surgeon cleans the junction between the appendix and the cecum, preparing the area for the application of a surgical stapler to secure the appendix. The appendix appears inflamed, influencing the choice of stapling over endo looping.
The surgeon exposes the junction of the appendix with the cecum, cleaning the area to create space for the stapler. The appendix is prepared for stapling, although an endo loop could also be used.
The surgeon cleans the surgical area and inspects the staple line on the appendix for any defects or injuries, confirming its integrity. The appendix is then placed into a retrieval bag.

The surgeon cleans the surgical area and inspects the staple line on the appendix for any defects or injuries. After confirming the integrity of the staple line, the appendix is placed into a retrieval bag.
The surgeon moves the appendix to the side and brings down the omentum to cover the staple line. The gallbladder appears secondarily inflamed, but this does not affect the procedure.
The surgeon moves the appendix to the side and brings the omentum down to cover the staple line. The gallbladder appears secondarily inflamed, but this does not affect the clinical outcome.
The surgeon removes the appendix from the body, completing the appendectomy procedure.
